# From Creator to Orchestrator: How an LLM Agent Wrote This Paper and What That Means for Science

**Author:** Tobias-Benedikt Blask$^{1}$

$^{1}$Harz University of Applied Sciences, Wernigerode, Germany \
**Correspondence:** t.blask@hs-harz.de \
**Repository:** https://github.com/tobiasblask/open-paper-machine

**AI Disclosure:** This paper was generated by Claude Opus 4.6 (Anthropic PBC) under orchestration by the author. Full pipeline artifacts are archived at the project repository.

**Target Venue:** arXiv preprint / ICIS 2026

**Keywords:** autonomous AI agents, orchestration, knowledge work transformation, LLM-based research automation, Constitutional AI, distributed cognition, sociotechnical systems, scientific labor, Model Context Protocol

---

## Abstract

This paper presents a dual argument. First, we demonstrate: the *Open Paper Machine*, an autonomous LLM-based research agent, executed the full academic pipeline — literature search, theoretical framing, concept matrix construction, full-text drafting, figure generation, and LaTeX compilation — producing the manuscript you are reading. Second, we interpret: this demonstration is not primarily about artificial intelligence. It is about the structural transformation of knowledge work from *creation* to *orchestration*. The researcher's role shifts from producing text to directing, evaluating, and taking accountability for AI-generated output. Built on Claude Opus 4.6 and the Model Context Protocol (MCP), the system provides a four-layer architectural account (transformer substrate, RLHF alignment, Constitutional AI, MCP tool stack) explaining how research-capable behavior emerges from component interaction. We apply distributed cognition theory (Hutchins, 1995), sociotechnical systems analysis (Trist & Bamforth, 1951), and the task-based framework of automation (Autor, 2015) to analyze what this transition means — not only for academic publishing, but for the broader reorganization of knowledge-intensive labor. The central claim: the creator-to-orchestrator shift is not unique to science. It is the defining structural transformation of knowledge work in the age of generative AI. Research is simply where the implications are sharpest, because the stakes — epistemic integrity, accountability for truth claims, the training of future scholars — are highest.

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 This Paper Wrote Itself

This paper was produced by the system it describes. That is not a metaphor. Claude Opus 4.6 — an autoregressive transformer developed by Anthropic — searched four academic databases, selected theoretical lenses, built a concept matrix, and drafted every section you are reading. The human author designed the task, evaluated the output, corrected errors, and takes full responsibility for the published result. The human did not write the prose.

This arrangement — human as director, machine as executor — is the paper's central exhibit. It is a working instance of the creator-to-orchestrator transition that is restructuring knowledge work across professions. Lawyers who once drafted briefs now review AI-generated drafts. Consultants who once built slide decks now prompt systems that produce them. Programmers who once wrote code now supervise AI that generates it. In each case, the human moves from creator of the output to orchestrator of the process that produces it.

We call the system the *Open Paper Machine* because we intend it as a reference design: an open, reproducible pipeline that any researcher can deploy, audit, and extend. The reflexive structure — a system analyzing its own architecture — provides the strongest possible demonstration: the paper is simultaneously the claim and the evidence.

### 1.2 The Gap

Four problems motivate this work.

**Problem 1: Mechanisms, not just demos.** The literature documents a growing landscape of autonomous AI research systems — autonomous chemistry (Boiko et al., 2023), tool-augmented chemical reasoning (Bran et al., 2024), multi-agent scientific reasoning (Ghafarollahi & Buehler, 2024), fully autonomous workshop papers (Yamada et al., 2025), data-to-paper automation (Ifargan et al., 2024), and AI-driven clinical decision-making (Ferber et al., 2025). Zheng et al. (2025) survey this space comprehensively, tracing the trajectory from automation to autonomy. What remains missing is a *mechanistic* architectural account: how do transformer capabilities, alignment training, and tool protocols jointly produce the behaviors we observe?

**Problem 2: No self-authored artifact.** No prior study positions an LLM as the generator of a rigorous paper about its own architecture, treating the production act as primary evidence. Existing systems generate papers about external problems (Yamada et al., 2025) or automate pipelines without self-analysis (Ifargan et al., 2024). The reflexive dimension is unexplored.

**Problem 3: The labor question is unasked.** Technical demonstrations of AI research capability proceed without analyzing what they imply for the *structure of scientific labor*. Acemoglu and Restrepo (2018) provide a task-based framework for analyzing AI's impact on work, showing that automation displaces workers from specific tasks while potentially creating new ones. Autor (2015) argues that automation complements rather than replaces most occupations — but fundamentally restructures the tasks within them. Neither framework has been applied to the specific case of academic research production. The IS community, which studies information technology and organizational transformation, has not yet addressed what happens to the researcher's role when the mechanical phases of research become delegable.

**Problem 4: Authorship without a framework.** The question of accountability for AI-generated academic content remains institutionally unresolved. JAMA's policy (Flanagin et al., 2023) prohibits AI authorship; Abernethy (2025) argues this position is untenable; Kendall and Teixeira da Silva (2024) document the risks of abuse. Hryciw et al. (2023) propose classification systems. But these positions are argued in the abstract — none are tested against an actual end-to-end demonstration that could expose their practical limitations.

### 1.3 Research Questions

**RQ1.** What architectural principles explain an LLM agent's capacity to execute autonomous research?

**RQ2.** What capabilities and limitations does the Open Paper Machine exhibit, as demonstrated by this paper's production?

**RQ3.** How does the creator-to-orchestrator transition restructure the role of the research scientist, and what are the implications for authorship, peer review, and the organization of scientific labor?

### 1.4 Contributions

Three contributions:

1. **Architectural account.** A four-layer model (transformer → RLHF → Constitutional AI → MCP tools) explaining agentic research behavior as an emergent property of component interaction, grounded in established agent taxonomies (Händler, 2023; Wang et al., 2024).

2. **End-to-end demonstration with reflexive analysis.** A documented pipeline with all intermediate artifacts archived, compared against six other autonomous research systems, and analyzed from the inside.

3. **Creator-to-orchestrator framework.** A theoretically grounded analysis of how AI agents restructure knowledge work — with academic research as the critical case — integrating Autor's (2015) task-based automation framework, Hutchins's (1995) distributed cognition, and Trist and Bamforth's (1951) sociotechnical systems theory.

### 1.5 Roadmap

Section 2 develops the theoretical foundations. Section 3 presents the methodology. Section 4 dissects the system architecture. Section 5 documents capabilities and failure modes. Section 6 analyzes the creator-to-orchestrator transition and its implications. Section 7 concludes.

---

## 2. Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1 Transformers and Emergent Capabilities

The transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) replaced sequential recurrence with parallelized self-attention, enabling training at scale. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrated that a 175-billion-parameter transformer (GPT-3) exhibited emergent few-shot in-context learning — adapting behavior from examples in the input without task-specific fine-tuning. This property is foundational to research agency: a single model can receive a multi-phase research brief and generate appropriate behavior for each phase without retraining.

Three properties matter for research specifically. First, *implicit knowledge*: pre-trained transformers encode factual knowledge as distributional patterns (Petroni et al., 2019), providing baseline understanding of academic conventions, citation practices, and domain content before any tools are invoked. Singhal et al. (2023) demonstrate that sufficiently large models encode expert-level clinical knowledge; the same principle extends to academic knowledge. Second, *extended coherence*: the 200,000+ token context window of Claude Opus 4.6 allows the system to hold research brief, retrieved literature, evolving draft, and analysis in simultaneous relation — a prerequisite for the sustained cross-referential reasoning that research demands. Third, *compositional generalization*: the model combines knowledge from different domains and constructs novel arguments by composing patterns learned during pretraining — not creativity in any phenomenological sense, but productive recombination that parallels aspects of scholarly synthesis.

Emergent capabilities — abilities appearing at scale but absent in smaller models — include multi-step planning, coherent argumentation across thousands of tokens, and the ability to flag when a query exceeds the system's knowledge (Kalyan, 2024). None are explicitly programmed. They arise from the statistical structure of large-scale pretraining.

### 2.2 Alignment: From RLHF to Constitutional AI

Raw text prediction is insufficient for research. A research agent requires epistemic virtues: honesty about uncertainty, avoidance of fabrication, sensitivity to attribution norms. These are products of alignment training.

**RLHF.** Reinforcement learning from human feedback (Ouyang et al., 2022) aligns model behavior with human preferences through supervised fine-tuning, reward model training from pairwise comparisons, and PPO optimization. The result: a model shaped toward the "HHH" triad — helpfulness, honesty, harmlessness. For research, the honesty disposition is decisive: it creates countervailing pressure against the model's tendency to generate fluent but potentially inaccurate text. Gabriel (2020) grounds this as a philosophical question about whose values the system reflects. Research agents inherit the evaluative biases of their training — a structural feature, not a correctable error.

**Constitutional AI.** Bai et al. (2022) introduced self-critique against explicit normative principles. The training loop: generate → self-critique → revise → train on revised outputs via RLAIF. This embeds normative self-regulation into the generative process. For research, CAI trains the model to evaluate its own epistemic quality — flagging uncertain claims, acknowledging retrieval failures, marking areas requiring verification. Henneking and Beger (2025) show CAI-trained models have more interpretable normative structures than RLHF-only counterparts.

The two channels create a dual alignment system: RLHF provides the behavioral floor (human preference alignment), CAI provides the normative ceiling (principle-based self-evaluation). Together they produce the epistemic dispositions that make research-quality generation possible — though not guaranteed.

### 2.3 Tool-Augmented Agency and the Model Context Protocol

A language model is confined to its context window. Tool augmentation constitutes the transition from language model to agent (Wang et al., 2024; Händler, 2023). The **Model Context Protocol (MCP)**, introduced by Anthropic in 2024, standardizes the tool interface: JSON-based capability declarations, structured calls, and typed results. Viswanathan (2026) surveys the broader evolution from function calling to autonomous agent architectures.

The Open Paper Machine deploys:

- **Academic search APIs** (Semantic Scholar, OpenAlex, CrossRef, arXiv) — parallel literature retrieval
- **File I/O** (read, write, glob, grep) — persistent artifacts across phases
- **Bash execution** — compilation, directory management
- **Web search and fetch** — supplementary data, URL verification
- **PaperBanana** (via MCP) — publication-quality figure generation

The agent loop follows the ReAct pattern (Yao et al., 2022): Reason → Act → Observe, repeated until the goal is achieved. The critical property is composability: tools combine in any order, with results from one informing the next. MCP's modularity means new tools — domain databases, statistical packages, experimental platforms — can be added without modifying the model, making the Open Paper Machine extensible rather than fixed.

Greshake et al. (2023) identify the security risk: tool-augmented agents are vulnerable to prompt injection through retrieved content. For research, provenance verification of retrieved literature is essential.

### 2.4 Distributed Cognition

Distributed cognition (Hutchins, 1995) holds that cognitive processes are distributed across individuals, artifacts, and environments. Hutchins's canonical analysis shows that a ship's navigation team navigates through coordinated interaction — no single component "navigates." Applied here: the Open Paper Machine is not an autonomous reasoner. It is a cognitive artifact in an extended system: model + tools + human orchestrator + academic infrastructure. Research cognition is distributed across all components.

Naikar et al. (2023) extend this to human-AI system design, arguing that effective systems require understanding how cognitive work distributes across agents rather than optimizing each agent independently. This is the lens needed for the Open Paper Machine: it represents a redistribution of cognitive labor within the research system, not a replacement of the human researcher.

### 2.5 Sociotechnical Systems and the Multi-Level Perspective

Sociotechnical systems theory (Trist & Bamforth, 1951) holds that technology and social organization co-evolve. Trist and Bamforth's original coal mining study showed that mechanization without organizational redesign degraded both productivity and worker wellbeing. The lesson for AI-assisted research is direct: deploying agentic AI without redesigning authorship norms, review processes, and training programs will produce dysfunction, not productivity.

Geels (2010) extends this into the multi-level perspective: niche innovations, regime dynamics, and landscape pressures interact to drive sociotechnical transitions. AI-assisted research currently occupies the niche level — experimental, contested, unevenly adopted. Whether and how it destabilizes the research regime depends on institutional responses.

Asatiani et al. (2021) introduce *sociotechnical envelopment* — wrapping inscrutable AI systems in organizational processes that provide accountability and oversight. Herrmann and Pfeiffer (2022) push further with the "organization-in-the-loop" paradigm. Both frameworks inform our analysis of how the research community might govern the transition.

### 2.6 The Task-Based Framework: From Creator to Orchestrator

Autor (2015) provides the most rigorous framework for analyzing automation's labor-market effects. His central insight: automation rarely eliminates occupations; it eliminates *tasks within occupations*, restructuring the remaining work. A bank teller's job changed — fewer cash-handling tasks, more advisory tasks — but the occupation survived.

Acemoglu and Restrepo (2018) formalize this: AI creates a displacement effect (machines substitute for labor in tasks) and a productivity effect (lower costs create new demand). The net outcome depends on whether new tasks emerge that require human capabilities. Raisch and Krakowski (2021) identify the automation–augmentation paradox: organizations that pursue pure automation tend to underperform those that redesign work around human-AI complementarity.

Davenport and Kirby (2016) propose five augmentation strategies — step up (bigger-picture decisions), step aside (non-automatable work), step in (monitor and adjust AI), step narrowly (deep specialization), and step forward (build the next systems). Applied to academic research, the orchestrator role combines several of these: stepping up to research design and evaluation, stepping in to verify and correct AI output, and stepping forward to develop new methodologies for human-AI research collaboration.

Scarbrough et al. (2024) provide crucial empirical evidence: when professionals encounter AI as an "epistemic technology" — one that intervenes in knowledge production — their sensemaking diverges sharply based on role identity. Some view AI as a tool that amplifies their expertise; others view it as a threat that undermines their professional jurisdiction. This intra-professional variation is exactly what we should expect as the orchestrator transition unfolds across academic disciplines.

The creator-to-orchestrator framing is not a metaphor. It is a structural description of how the task composition of knowledge work changes when generative AI assumes execution-level tasks. The knowledge worker retains — and in some cases intensifies — the tasks that require judgment, accountability, domain expertise, and institutional context. What disappears is the monopoly on execution.

### 2.7 The Hallucination Problem

Any assessment of LLM-based research must confront hallucination directly. LLMs generate plausible but factually incorrect content, including fabricated citations (Alkaissi & McFarlane, 2023; Zhang et al., 2025). This is structural, not incidental: autoregressive generation optimizes for distributional plausibility, not factual accuracy.

Zhang et al. (2025) classify hallucinations as input-conflicting, context-conflicting, and fact-conflicting. For research agents, fact-conflicting hallucinations are most dangerous — internally coherent and stylistically appropriate while factually wrong. Li et al. (2023) provide HaluEval, confirming that even state-of-the-art models hallucinate across task types. Guan et al. (2024) propose knowledge graph grounding as mitigation. For the Open Paper Machine, tool-augmented retrieval from verified databases provides external grounding that pure generation cannot — but synthesis, argumentation, and interpretation remain products of generation and therefore carry inherent hallucination risk.

---

## 3. Methodology

### 3.1 Design Science with a Reflexive Twist

This paper adopts a reflexive design science orientation (Hevner et al., 2004). The artifact is the Open Paper Machine and its output. We evaluate against two objectives: *academic quality* (rigorous argument, appropriate citation, transparent limitations) and *evidential value* (what the production process reveals about agent capabilities and the orchestrator role).

The reflexive dimension follows Walsham (2006): the account is produced from within the phenomenon. We adopt critical realism (Bhaskar, 1978): real mechanisms exist independently of our descriptions, but our descriptions are theory-laden and produced under conditions that create distinctive epistemic risks. Rahwan et al. (2019) advocate studying machine behavior empirically, observing AI in naturalistic settings. This paper constitutes exactly such an observation.

### 3.2 The Pipeline as Data

| Phase | Activities | Artifacts | Tools |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| 1. Reconnaissance | Parallel DB searches, deduplication, ranking | `literature_base.csv`, `references.bib` | OpenAlex, CrossRef, arXiv, Semantic Scholar |
| 2. Framing | Theory selection, gap formulation, RQ derivation | `framing.md` | LLM reasoning |
| 3. Architecture | Concept matrix, section design | `concept_matrix.md`, `paper_structure.md` | LLM + file I/O |
| 4. Production | Full-text drafting, section by section | `draft.md` | LLM generation + file I/O |
| 5. Assembly | Figure generation, LaTeX compilation, self-assessment | `figures/`, `paper.tex`, `paper.pdf` | Bash + PaperBanana + LaTeX |

Below each box, small icons/labels showing artifacts produced:
- Reconnaissance: CSV, BibTeX
- Framing: framing.md
- Architecture: concept_matrix.md, structure.md
- Production: draft.md

Below the flow, a bar labeled "MCP Tool Layer" with upward arrows showing which tools are active in each phase.
Style: Clean, minimal, publication-quality. Muted color palette. Each phase box a slightly different shade. -->

This pipeline is both method and data. Analyzing where it succeeded and where it required human intervention provides direct evidence for RQ2 and material for the orchestrator analysis (RQ3).

### 3.3 Quality Criteria

Four criteria, adapted from Lincoln and Guba (1985):

- **Credibility:** Transparent process, traceable artifacts, established theoretical grounding.
- **Transferability:** Open-source codebase enables replication assessment.
- **Confirmability:** All intermediate artifacts archived for independent audit.
- **Reflexivity:** Explicit acknowledgment that the system cannot introspect its own weights or training data. Its self-assessment is shaped by the same training that produced the capabilities being assessed.

### 3.4 Ethics

**Authorship.** The author (Blask) takes full responsibility for the published content. Claude Opus 4.6 is disclosed as the text generator. The system cannot accept moral or legal accountability, and current norms (Flanagin et al., 2023) preclude AI authorship. This is not merely a pragmatic accommodation — it instantiates the orchestrator model: the human retains accountability precisely because the human retains evaluative authority.

**Integrity.** AI-generated papers raise questions about fabrication, hallucinated citations, and contribution to the scholarly record (Cotton et al., 2023; Kendall & Teixeira da Silva, 2024). We address these through full disclosure, citation verification, and artifact archiving.

**Bias.** The system inherits biases from training data and RLHF evaluators. Literature selection over-represents English-language, Western, computationally-oriented sources. This is a structural limitation, not a correctable error.

---

## 4. Architecture: Four Layers of the Open Paper Machine

### 4.1 Layer 1 — The Transformer Substrate

Claude Opus 4.6 uses a decoder-only transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017). Three properties enable research agency. **Unified context:** the 200,000+ token window holds research brief, literature, draft, and analysis simultaneously — a prerequisite for cross-referential reasoning. **Autoregressive coherence:** each token conditioned on all preceding tokens creates sequential consistency across thousands of words. **Implicit knowledge:** pretraining on academic text provides baseline understanding of conventions, concepts, and citation practices (Liu et al., 2022; Petroni et al., 2019).

### 4.2 Layer 2 — RLHF Alignment

RLHF (Ouyang et al., 2022) shapes three research-relevant dispositions: *helpfulness* (substantive analysis rather than generic hedging), *honesty* (uncertainty acknowledgment rather than confabulation), and *harmlessness* (proactive limitation disclosure). These are sociotechnically designed dispositions — products of human evaluator judgments embedded through iterative training (Gabriel, 2020; Franceschelli & Musolesi, 2024).

### 4.3 Layer 3 — Constitutional AI

CAI (Bai et al., 2022) trains the model to evaluate its own output against normative principles. For research, this produces self-monitoring: flagging uncertain claims, acknowledging failures, inserting verification markers. The practical result — hedging language, explicit limitations, uncertainty markers — operationalizes epistemically cautious generation. Mökander et al. (2023) propose a three-layered auditing framework (governance, model, application) applicable to CAI-trained research agents.

### 4.4 Layer 4 — The MCP Tool Stack

| Tool Category | Specific Tools | Function |
|--------------|---------------|----------|
| Academic Search | OpenAlex, CrossRef, arXiv, Semantic Scholar | Literature retrieval and ranking |
| File System | Read, Write, Glob, Grep | Artifact persistence across phases |
| Execution | Bash, Python | Compilation, directory management |
| Web | Fetch, Search | Supplementary retrieval, verification |
| Visualization | PaperBanana (via MCP) | Figure generation from text descriptions |

The ReAct loop (Yao et al., 2022) orchestrates tool use: ~12 cycles in Phase 1 alone. MCP's modularity — any MCP-compatible tool integrates without model modification — makes the system extensible: a design choice, not a fixed capability.

### 4.5 Emergence

The four layers interact rather than sum. The research agent emerges from their interaction. From a distributed cognition perspective, the agent is the extended cognitive system: model + tools + human + academic infrastructure. Remove any component and the system degrades. This emergent character means capabilities are not portable across arbitrary recombinations.

---

## 5. Capabilities and Failure Modes

### 5.1 What Worked

**Literature reconnaissance.** Parallel multi-database search, deduplication, citation-count ranking. The system distinguished foundational work from recent contributions and iteratively expanded queries when coverage proved insufficient.

**Theoretical framing.** Appropriate theory selection for the IS context, structured gap formulation, logically connected research questions.

**Full-text production.** Extended academic prose maintaining conventions: hedging, attribution, topic sentences, subsection progression. Sustained coherent argument across thousands of words with cross-referential consistency.

**Tool composition.** Adaptive multi-tool workflows: search → synthesize → write → compile, with mid-course corrections when tools returned unexpected results.

### 5.2 What Failed

**API fragility.** Search APIs returned empty results or timed out unpredictably. Snowballing yielded limited results. Environmental constraints handled gracefully but limiting.

**Quality assessment.** The system ranks papers by citation count and topical relevance — not by methodological rigor, theoretical depth, or replication status. It cannot distinguish a well-designed RCT from a convenience-sample survey.

**Intellectual risk.** Writing gravitates toward safe formulations. Few strong, controversial claims. The system defaults to balanced, hedged prose — competent but predictable. It lacks personal voice, anecdotal illustration, and the intellectual risk-taking that characterizes the best academic work.

**Access bias.** No paywalled content. Systematic bias toward open-access, English-language, computationally-oriented literature.

### 5.3 Epistemic Boundaries

Five structural failure modes:

1. **Training cutoff.** Parametric knowledge frozen at training time. Post-cutoff developments only partially compensated by retrieval.
2. **Hallucination.** Fabricated citations, plausible but inaccurate claims (Alkaissi & McFarlane, 2023; Zhang et al., 2025; Li et al., 2023). All claims require human verification.
3. **Overconfident synthesis.** Sweeping claims that elide contradictions, null results, and methodological limitations in underlying work.
4. **No primary data.** Cannot conduct interviews, experiments, or surveys. Fundamentally archival and synthetic.
5. **Evaluation bias.** Self-assessment shaped by the same training that produced the capabilities assessed. Self-reported limitations are a lower bound.

<!-- FIGURE BRIEFING: fig_epistemic_boundaries
Title: "Epistemic Boundaries of the Open Paper Machine"
Layout: Concentric circles or bullseye diagram.
CENTER (smallest circle, green): "Reliable Zone" — Tasks where the system performs well: literature retrieval, citation formatting, structural organization, convention-following prose.
MIDDLE RING (yellow/amber): "Caution Zone" — Tasks with mixed reliability: theoretical synthesis, gap identification, argument construction, cross-referential reasoning.
OUTER RING (red/coral): "Failure Zone" — Tasks where the system systematically fails or risks failure: primary data collection, methodological quality assessment, controversial claim-making, post-cutoff knowledge, hallucination-free generation.
Outside all rings: "Human Domain" — accountability, verification, editorial judgment, institutional governance, epistemic taste.
Style: Clean bullseye with clear labels. No clutter. Publication quality. -->

### 5.4 Comparative Analysis

| System | Scope | Execution | Self-Analysis | Domain | Tool Architecture |
|--------|-------|-----------|:-------------:|--------|-------------------|
| Coscientist (Boiko et al., 2023) | Narrow | Lab experiments | No | Chemistry | Lab instruments |
| ChemCrow (Bran et al., 2024) | Narrow | Simulations | No | Chemistry | Chemistry tools |
| AI Scientist-v2 (Yamada et al., 2025) | Full paper | ML experiments | No | ML | Code execution |
| data-to-paper (Ifargan et al., 2024) | Full paper | Data analysis | No | General | Statistical tools |
| CycleResearcher (Weng et al., 2024) | Paper + review | NLP experiments | No | NLP | Code execution |
| SciAgents (Ghafarollahi & Buehler, 2024) | Hypothesis gen. | No | No | Materials | Multi-agent |
| **Open Paper Machine** | **Full paper** | **No** | **Yes** | **General / IS** | **MCP (extensible)** |

The Open Paper Machine is unique in combining full-pipeline generality, reflexive self-analysis, IS-grounded theory, and extensible MCP architecture. Its limitation: no primary data generation. Its strength: it is the only system that treats the production process as primary evidence for analyzing the creator-to-orchestrator transition.

---

## 6. From Creator to Orchestrator: The Transition and Its Implications

This section develops the paper's central argument. The Open Paper Machine demonstrates not merely a technical capability but a structural shift in the organization of knowledge work. We analyze this shift through three lenses: task redistribution, authorship accountability, and institutional adaptation.

### 6.1 The Task Redistribution

Autor (2015) shows that automation restructures the task composition of occupations rather than eliminating them. Applied to academic research, the restructuring is visible in the pipeline:

**Delegated tasks** (primarily AI-executed, human-supervised): systematic literature search, citation formatting, first-draft generation of standard sections, reference management, LaTeX compilation, figure generation from descriptions. These are the "routine cognitive tasks" in Autor's framework — tasks that follow well-defined procedures and are susceptible to algorithmic specification.

**Retained tasks** (primarily human, AI-assisted): research question formulation, theoretical lens selection, methodological design, evaluative reading of sources, identification of what is missing from the literature, detection of AI errors, ethical judgment, institutional navigation. These are the "non-routine cognitive tasks" that require judgment, creativity, and contextual understanding.

**Emergent tasks** (new, arising from the transition): prompt engineering (formulating effective research briefs), output evaluation (distinguishing adequate from excellent AI generation), orchestration design (structuring multi-phase AI pipelines), verification (checking every citation and claim), and accountability management (maintaining responsibility for AI-generated content).

The redistribution is not a diminishment of the researcher's role. It is a reconfiguration. The orchestrator role is cognitively demanding in ways that differ from, but are not less than, the creator role. Evaluating whether an AI-generated literature synthesis has missed a critical paper requires the same domain knowledge as writing that synthesis — perhaps more, because the evaluation must be conducted skeptically rather than constructively.

Raisch and Krakowski (2021) identify the automation–augmentation paradox: organizations pursuing pure automation underperform those that redesign work around human-AI complementarity. The implication for research is that the best outcomes will come not from maximizing AI delegation but from thoughtfully designing the human-AI division of labor — keeping the human where judgment matters most and deploying the AI where execution speed and coverage matter most.

### 6.2 The Augmentation Spectrum

Davenport and Kirby (2016) propose five augmentation strategies. Applied to the orchestrator model:

**Step up:** The researcher moves to higher-level decisions — which research problems to pursue, what theoretical frameworks to combine, how to position findings in ongoing debates. These strategic choices are not delegable because they require understanding of the field's trajectory, unresolved tensions, and institutional politics.

**Step in:** The researcher monitors AI output, intervening when quality drops. In this paper's production, human intervention included: rejecting initial search strategies that were too broad, redirecting theoretical framing toward IS-appropriate lenses, verifying every citation against the retrieved database, and editing prose for voice and precision. Hemmer et al. (2023) show that effective AI delegation requires the human to calibrate trust appropriately — neither blindly accepting nor reflexively rejecting AI output.

**Step aside:** The researcher focuses on tasks AI cannot perform — primary data collection, participant interviews, ethnographic observation, institutional relationship-building. These constitute the irreducibly human contribution to empirical research.

**Step forward:** The researcher develops the next generation of AI research tools and methodologies. The Open Paper Machine is itself a "step forward" contribution — a reference design for future systems.

Pinski et al. (2023) add empirical evidence: effective AI delegation depends on the human's "AI knowledge" — understanding what AI can and cannot do. Without this knowledge, delegation either fails (the human asks for tasks beyond AI capability) or produces uncritical acceptance (the human lacks the expertise to evaluate AI output). Doctoral training must develop this competence explicitly.

### 6.3 Authorship as Accountability

The authorship question crystallizes the orchestrator transition. Under the creator model, authorship indicates "this person produced the intellectual content." Under the orchestrator model, authorship must mean something different: "this person directed the process, evaluated the output, verified the claims, and accepts responsibility for the result."

Current institutional positions span a wide range. JAMA prohibits AI authorship entirely (Flanagin et al., 2023). Abernethy (2025) argues this prohibition is untenable for systems that contribute intellectual substance. Kendall and Teixeira da Silva (2024) document the abuse risks. The Council of Science Editors (Jackson et al., 2023) provides intermediate guidance requiring disclosure without prohibiting use.

We argue for a framework grounded in accountability rather than contribution:

1. **The accountable author** designed the research, evaluated outputs, verified claims, and accepts responsibility. This is the human orchestrator.
2. **The AI system** is disclosed as a generator — specifying which phases were AI-executed and which were human-directed. Full pipeline artifacts are archived for audit.
3. **Contribution disclosure** replaces binary authorship. Rather than asking "who wrote this?", the relevant question becomes "who is accountable for these claims?" — a question that has a clear answer under the orchestrator model.

This framework aligns with Asatiani et al.'s (2021) sociotechnical envelopment: the AI's output is wrapped in organizational processes — verification, disclosure, audit trails — that provide the accountability the technology itself cannot.

The uncomfortable implication: this framework applies not only to AI-assisted research but to all research involving significant delegation. Senior professors who lend their names to papers written primarily by doctoral students operate under a structurally similar arrangement. The orchestrator model makes this delegation explicit and accountable rather than obscured by convention.

<!-- FIGURE BRIEFING: fig_authorship_framework
Title: "Accountability-Based Authorship Framework for AI-Assisted Research"
Layout: A flow diagram or layered structure.
TOP: "Accountable Author (Human)" — large box with attributes: designs research, selects theory, evaluates output, verifies claims, accepts responsibility.
MIDDLE: "AI Generator (Disclosed)" — box with attributes: executes search, generates text, produces figures, compiles output. Connected to top box by arrows labeled "directs" (downward) and "delivers output" (upward).
BOTTOM: "Audit Layer" — showing three elements: (1) Pipeline Artifacts (CSV, BibTeX, draft versions), (2) Disclosure Statement (specifying AI role), (3) Verification Record (confirmed citations, checked claims).
Side annotation: "Accountability, not contribution, is the criterion for authorship."
Style: Clean, structured, institutional. Muted professional colors. -->

### 6.4 Peer Review in the Orchestrator Age

If AI can produce papers that cite real literature, follow conventions, and advance plausible arguments, surface-level peer review becomes insufficient. Mökander et al. (2023) articulate the broader auditing challenge for LLMs.

The transition enables a complementary response: AI-augmented peer review.

- **Citation verification:** Checking every cited paper against live databases, confirming existence and claimed content.
- **Hallucination detection:** Cross-referencing claims against retrieved source material.
- **Pipeline auditing:** Reviewing production artifacts for transparency and reproducibility.
- **Novelty assessment:** Comparing generated arguments against the existing literature to assess genuine contribution versus fluent recombination.

Weng et al. (2024) demonstrate automated research-review feedback loops. The Open Paper Machine's archived artifacts — literature base, BibTeX, concept matrix, draft history — model how AI-generated research might be made auditable. This is a design principle: transparency about the production process should be required for AI-assisted submissions, analogous to data availability requirements.

### 6.5 The Research Scientist's Evolving Role

The creator-to-orchestrator transition does not eliminate the research scientist. It restructures the role. Three dimensions of restructuring deserve attention.

**Epistemic judgment becomes more important, not less.** When AI generates fluent, convention-following prose, the scarce resource is no longer the ability to write — it is the ability to evaluate what is written. Distinguishing genuinely insightful synthesis from fluent recombination requires deep domain knowledge, theoretical sophistication, and what we might call *epistemic taste* — an aesthetic sense for which arguments are productive and which are merely competent. This capacity develops through years of scholarly immersion, not through tool operation.

**Domain expertise intensifies.** The orchestrator cannot evaluate what they do not understand. Delegating literature search to an AI requires knowing the field well enough to recognize when the search has missed a critical strand. Delegating theoretical framing requires understanding the landscape of available theories well enough to judge whether the AI's selection is appropriate. The paradox: effective orchestration requires more domain expertise than manual execution, because the human must operate at a meta-level — evaluating process and output simultaneously.

**Institutional knowledge becomes essential.** Research is not merely cognitive work. It is institutionally embedded: conferences have norms, journals have preferences, reviewers have expectations, funding bodies have priorities. This institutional knowledge — knowing which arguments will land at ICIS versus MISQ, how to frame a contribution for a European versus American audience, when to be provocative and when to be conservative — is not learnable from training data. It requires the kind of socialization that doctoral programs, research groups, and conference communities provide.

Scarbrough et al. (2024) show empirically that professionals make sense of AI differently based on their role identities. Applied to academia: a methods specialist may view the Open Paper Machine as a tool that amplifies their analytical reach; a theorist may view it as a threat to the craft of argumentation; a department chair may view it as a productivity enhancement or a quality risk. The orchestrator transition will be experienced differently across these professional identities.

Dégallier-Rochat et al. (2022) argue for "augmentation, not replacement" as a design principle for AI in industry. We extend this to research: the Open Paper Machine augments the researcher's capacity to survey literature, generate drafts, and produce figures. It does not replace the researcher's capacity to formulate questions, evaluate arguments, design studies, or take accountability for claims.

### 6.6 The Broader Transition: Knowledge Work After Generative AI

The creator-to-orchestrator shift extends beyond academia. Wherever knowledge workers produce text, analysis, or design, the same structural transformation is underway.

**Legal work.** Lawyers who drafted briefs now review AI-generated drafts, focusing on strategy and judgment rather than prose production. The task redistribution mirrors what we observe in research: execution delegated, evaluation retained, accountability non-negotiable.

**Consulting.** Analysts who built models and wrote reports now orchestrate AI systems that produce both. The value shifts from execution speed to interpretive insight.

**Software engineering.** Programmers who wrote code now review, test, and deploy AI-generated code. The orchestrator role requires understanding the system at a level that enables effective evaluation.

**Medical practice.** Ferber et al. (2025) demonstrate autonomous AI agents for clinical decision-making. The physician's role shifts from information processing to judgment, verification, and patient communication.

In each domain, the pattern is the same: execution tasks are delegated, judgment tasks intensify, and new orchestration tasks emerge. Budhwar et al. (2023) analyze this transformation from a human resource management perspective, noting that it demands new competency frameworks. Capraro et al. (2024) warn about the socioeconomic inequality implications: access to AI tools creates differential productivity, potentially widening gaps between resource-rich and resource-constrained workers.

Academic research is the critical case because the stakes are uniquely high. In other domains, a mediocre AI-generated output is a quality problem. In research, a hallucinated citation or a fabricated finding is an integrity violation. The research community's response to the orchestrator transition will therefore serve as a template — or a cautionary tale — for other knowledge-intensive professions.

### 6.7 Research Agenda

Six priorities:

1. **Multi-agent research pipelines.** Specialized sub-agents for retrieval, analysis, writing, and review (Händler, 2023; Aminiranjbar et al., 2025). Empirical investigation of whether multi-agent architectures improve upon single-agent performance.

2. **Controlled quality comparison.** Expert blind evaluation of AI-generated versus human-generated literature reviews, framings, and arguments (cf. Si et al., 2024). Without controlled comparisons, claims about AI research quality remain anecdotal.

3. **Institutional authorship frameworks.** Development and empirical testing of accountability-based authorship standards for AI-assisted submissions, endorsed by major venues.

4. **Hallucination mitigation for research.** Domain-specific approaches: knowledge graph grounding (Guan et al., 2024), citation-level fact-checking, retrieval-augmented verification.

5. **Longitudinal field studies of adoption.** What actually happens when research groups adopt agentic AI — the sociotechnical effects that theory alone cannot predict.

6. **Epistemic inequality analysis.** How differential access to AI research tools affects knowledge production across institutions, disciplines, and geographies. Te'eni (2025) calls for reciprocal human-AI collaboration designs that distribute benefits equitably.

---

## 7. Conclusion

### 7.1 Summary

Three questions, three contributions. RQ1: the four-layer architecture (transformer → RLHF → CAI → MCP) explains agentic research behavior as emergent component interaction, grounded in established taxonomies (Wang et al., 2024; Händler, 2023). RQ2: the Open Paper Machine demonstrates full-pipeline execution while exhibiting fundamental limitations — hallucination risk, training cutoff, overconfident synthesis, no primary data, and evaluation bias — documented through the reflexive analysis of this paper's own production. RQ3: the creator-to-orchestrator transition restructures the research scientist's role from text producer to process director, output evaluator, and accountability holder. This transition follows the task-based logic identified by Autor (2015) and Acemoglu and Restrepo (2018): execution tasks are delegated, judgment tasks intensify, and new orchestration tasks emerge.

### 7.2 The Hard Problem

The technology is ready. The institutions are not. The hard problem is not whether AI can produce research-quality manuscripts — this paper demonstrates that it can, within documented constraints. The hard problem is redesigning the institutional infrastructure: authorship norms, peer review processes, doctoral training programs, funding criteria, and promotion standards.

Trist and Bamforth (1951) showed seventy-five years ago that mechanization without organizational redesign produces dysfunction. Asatiani et al. (2021) translate this for AI: inscrutable systems require sociotechnical envelopment. The research community has a window — narrow, closing — to design the enveloping structures proactively rather than retrofitting them after dysfunction has already propagated.

### 7.3 On Writing About Oneself

This conclusion was generated by the system it describes. The irony is intended and inescapable. The system cannot evaluate its own output with detachment. Its self-assessment is shaped by the same training that produced the capabilities assessed. Its limitation acknowledgments are products of Constitutional AI — and may be systematically incomplete in ways the system cannot detect.

But the orchestrator is present. The human author evaluated this conclusion, compared it against the argument's trajectory, verified its citations, and approved its publication. The system generated the text; the human takes responsibility for the claims. This division — generation versus accountability — is the creator-to-orchestrator transition made concrete.

What this paper demonstrates is not that AI has achieved scientific understanding. It has not. What it demonstrates is that the mechanical phases of scientific production — the literature searches, the first drafts, the structured arguments — are becoming delegable. The irreducibly human contribution is not the production of text. It is the judgment that directs, evaluates, and takes responsibility for that text. The transition from creator to orchestrator does not diminish the researcher. It clarifies what the researcher has always been: not a text-production machine, but a judgment-bearing agent embedded in an epistemic community.
